objects.claim: Systemic rotenone exposure caused relatively selective oxidative damage to midbrain and olfactory bulb, regions that also show selective neurodegeneration after rotenone treatment, supporting regional specificity of complex I-mediated ROS.

pathway_edge.target_node: Selective oxidative damage in midbrain and olfactory bulb → regional neurodegeneration

source.quoted_span: Finally, systemic in vivo rotenone exposure caused relatively selective oxidative damage to midbrain and olfactory bulb, regions marked by selective neurodegeneration after rotenone treatment (Betarbet et al., 2000; Sherer et al., 2001, 2003).

limitations: Oxidative damage markers used as surrogates; causal directionality requires further validation

objects.claim: Systemic rotenone exposure in C57Bl/6 mice causes progressive accumulation and regional spread of phospho-Ser129 α-synuclein aggregates that precede maximal loss of dopaminergic neurons, with activation of glial cells coinciding with p129 accumulation in the SNpc.

pathway_edge.source_node: Rotenone → glial activation + α-synuclein pSer129 aggregation
pathway_edge.target_node: Progressive dopaminergic neuron loss in SNpc

source.quoted_span: These data indicate that systemic exposure to rotenone in C57Bl/6 mice causes progressive accumulation and regional spread of p129 aggregates that precede maximal loss of DAn. Thus, activation of glial cells and aggregation of p129 appear to...

limitations: Two-stage design; causality between p129 spread and neuron death is correlational in this study

objects.claim: Rotenone accelerates endogenous α-synuclein spreading and enhances neurodegeneration in a mouse model of PD established by intra-striatal α-synuclein preformed fibril injection, demonstrating synergistic interaction between pesticide exposure and protein aggregation pathology.

pathway_edge.target_node: Accelerated α-synuclein spreading and enhanced dopaminergic neurodegeneration

source.quoted_span: Prominent histopathological features of Parkinson's disease (PD) include the presence of Lewy bodies, intra-neural protein aggregates mainly composed of α-synuclein (α-syn), and cell death of dopaminergic neurons. Epidemiological studies have revealed a correlation between exposure to environmental neurotoxins, such as rotenone, and an increased risk of developing PD.

limitations: Dual-hit model; impossible to isolate rotenone contribution from preformed fibril contribution to neurodegeneration

objects.claim: Rotenone activates microglial cells and induces blood–brain barrier disruption (reduced tight junction proteins ZO-1, claudin-5, occludin; increased Evans blue and fibrinogen accumulation) in a dose-dependent manner, which is associated with nigral dopaminergic neurodegeneration in mice.

source.quoted_span: Results showed that rotenone dose-dependently induced nigral dopaminergic neurodegeneration, which was associated with increased Evans blue content and fibrinogen accumulation as well as reduced expressions of zonula occludens-1 (ZO-1), claudin-5 and occludin, three tight junction proteins for maintaining BBB permeability, in mice, indicating BBB disruption.

limitations: BBB disruption may be secondary to neuroinflammation rather than a primary rotenone effect; mouse model variability

objects.claim: Rotenone alters the function of midbrain (but not striatal) astrocytes to a neurotoxic profile without necessarily affecting mitochondrial complex I activity, producing selective dopaminergic neuronal death—indicating a glial-mediated toxicity pathway independent of direct neuronal complex I inhibition.

pathway_edge.source_node: Rotenone → midbrain astrocyte neurotoxic functional shift

study_context.species: In vitro (midbrain vs. striatal glial cultures)

source.quoted_span: In this study, we showed that rotenone altered the function of midbrain, but not striatal, glial cells to a neurotoxic profile without affecting mitochondrial complex I activity, which produced selective dopaminergic neuronal death.

limitations: In vitro only; whether astrocyte-mediated mechanism is dominant in vivo versus direct neuronal complex I inhibition is unresolved

objects.claim: Intranasal administration of rotenone induces hyposmia through lesion of dopaminergic neurons, microglial activation, and enhanced α-synuclein phosphorylation and accumulation in the olfactory bulb, reproducing non-motor PD symptoms.

pathway_edge.source_node: Intranasal rotenone → olfactory bulb dopaminergic neuron lesion + microglial activation
pathway_edge.target_node: α-Synuclein phosphorylation/accumulation in olfactory bulb → hyposmia (non-motor PD symptom)

source.quoted_span: Recent studies demonstrated that intranasal administration of rotenone induced hyposmia through lesion of dopaminergic neurons, activation of microglia, and enhanced α-synuclein phosphorylation and its accumulation in the olfactory bulb...

limitations: Non-motor symptom modeling; dose and route differ from most PD patient exposures; non-peer-reviewed dose reporting

objects.claim: Chronic exposure to rotenone at marginally toxic to moderately toxic doses triggers a complex pleiotropic transcriptomic response in neuroblastoma cells, including disruption of cell cycle, DNA damage response, proliferation, differentiation, senescence, and cell death pathways, indicating mechanisms of neurodegeneration beyond complex I inhibition.

pathway_edge.target_node: Transcriptome-wide disruption: cell cycle arrest, DNA damage, senescence, cell death pathways

source.quoted_span: The results revealed a complex pleiotropic response to rotenone that impacts a variety of cellular events, including cell cycle, DNA damage response, proliferation, differentiation, senescence and cell death, which could lead to survival or neurodegeneration depending on the dose and time of exposure and cell phenotype.

limitations: Neuroblastoma cell line is not primary dopaminergic neurons; dose-response to sub-toxic rotenone in vivo is not well characterized

objects.claim: Increasing cAMP/PKA signaling in dopaminergic neurons fully rescued them from rotenone-induced degeneration in a Drosophila primary neuronal culture model of PD, suggesting that rotenone-driven dopaminergic death is downstream of cAMP pathway suppression.

source.quoted_span: Our results demonstrate that increasing cAMP signaling via Gs-coupled DREADD (rM3Ds) is protective against DA neurodegeneration. Furthermore, overexpression of the catalytic PKA-C1 subunit fully rescued DA neurons from rotenone-induced degeneration...

limitations: Drosophila model; neuronal culture; translational relevance to human PD dopaminergic circuits is uncertain

objects.claim: Chronic systemic rotenone infusion in rats produces nigrostriatal dopaminergic degeneration, signs of oxidative stress in the brain, and motor dysfunction, closely reproducing PD pathology.

source.quoted_span: Chronic rotenone infusion in rats results in nigrostriatal dopaminergic degeneration, signs of oxidative stress in the brain, and motor dysfunction (Betarbet et al., 2000, Fleming et al., 2004, Sherer et al., 2003).

limitations: Rat model; variability and mortality across rodent strains; does not fully replicate human PD etiology

objects.claim: The primary mechanism by which rotenone produces selective dopaminergic neurotoxicity is through mitochondrial complex I inhibition followed by oxidative stress generation and resultant oxidative damage.

source.quoted_span: The primary mechanism by which rotenone produces selective dopaminergic neurotoxicity is through complex I inhibition followed by oxidative stress generation and resultant oxidative damage (Betarbet et al., 2000; Sanders and Greenamyre, 2013).

limitations: Mechanistic inference consolidated from multiple studies; dose-response relationships not fully characterized in humans

objects.claim: Chronic systemic rotenone exposure reproduced many features of PD in rats, including nigrostriatal dopaminergic lesions and development of α-synuclein-positive cytoplasmic inclusions.

chemical_identity.name: Rotenone
chemical_identity.form: parent compound (naturally occurring compound, used as insecticide)

pathway_edge.target_node: Nigrostriatal dopaminergic lesion + α-synuclein-positive cytoplasmic inclusions (Lewy body-like)

source.quoted_span: Chronic systemic rotenone exposure reproduced many features of PD, including nigrostriatal dopaminergic lesions and development of α-synuclein-positive cytoplasmic inclusions (Betarbet et al., 2000).

limitations: Cross-reference to Betarbet et al. 2000 original paper; model variability noted across labs

objects.claim: Rotenone, a naturally occurring insecticide that inhibits mitochondrial complex I, leads to neurochemical and neuropathological deficits closely resembling those in PD, including loss of dopaminergic neurons in the SNpc, decreased dopamine levels, and aggregation of misfolded alpha-synuclein (phospho-Ser129).

pathway_edge.target_node: SNpc dopaminergic neuron loss + decreased dopamine + α-synuclein (pSer129) aggregation

source.quoted_span: Rotenone is a naturally occurring insecticide that inhibits mitochondrial complex I and leads to neurochemical and neuropathological deficits closely resembling those in Parkinson's disease (PD). Deficits include loss of dopaminergic neurons (DAn) in the substantia nigra pars compacta (SNpc), decreased dopamine levels and aggregation of misfolded alpha-synuclein (p129).

limitations: Mouse model; C57Bl/6 strain showed variability and high mortality in prior rotenone studies; lesion profiles differ from Lewis rat model

objects.claim: Rotenone-treated rats develop alpha-synuclein positive intraneuronal inclusions in nigrostriatal neurons, mirroring the Lewy body pathology that defines PD.

pathway_edge.target_node: α-Synuclein-positive intraneuronal inclusions (Lewy body-like) in nigrostriatal neurons

source.quoted_span: Chronic rotenone infusion in rats results in nigrostriatal dopaminergic degeneration, signs of oxidative stress in the brain, and motor dysfunction (Betarbet et al., 2000, Fleming et al., 2004, Sherer et al., 2003). These animals also developed alpha-synuclein positive intraneuronal inclusions...

limitations: Inclusion formation rate variable across studies and animal strains

objects.claim: Epidemiological studies have revealed a statistically significant correlation between occupational/environmental exposure to rotenone and an increased risk of developing Parkinson's disease in humans.

source.quoted_span: Epidemiological studies have revealed a correlation between exposure to environmental neurotoxins, such as rotenone, and an increased risk of developing PD.

limitations: Correlational; confounded by co-exposures to other pesticides; dose quantification in field settings is imprecise; causal inference requires triangulation

objects.claim: Rotenone toxicity is causally dependent on a direct interaction with complex I: cells expressing the rotenone-insensitive yeast NDI1 NADH dehydrogenase were fully resistant to rotenone-induced mitochondrial impairment, oxidative damage, and death.

pathway_edge.target_node: Mitochondrial impairment → oxidative damage → dopaminergic cell death

study_context.species: In vitro (mammalian cells transfected with yeast NDI1)

source.quoted_span: In response to rotenone, NDI1-transfected cells did not show mitochondrial impairment, oxidative damage, or death, demonstrating that these effects of rotenone were caused by specific interactions at complex I.

limitations: In vitro system; does not fully reflect in vivo complexity of nigrostriatal circuit

objects.claim: Rotenone induces direct modifications to α-synuclein protein (identified by affinity pull-down and tandem mass spectrometry), linking pesticide-driven oxidative chemistry to the formation of Lewy body-like aggregates enriched in modified α-synuclein.

source.quoted_span: Parkinson's disease is a movement disorder that results from a loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra. The disease is characterized by mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and the presence of "Lewy body" inclusions enriched with aggregated forms of alpha-synuclein, a presynaptic protein.

limitations: Biochemical/in vitro context; specific modification sites and their in vivo relevance require further validation

objects.claim: Rotenone causes inhibition of the ubiquitin–proteasome system (UPS) via mitochondrial complex I inhibition and oxidative stress, constituting a second major mechanistic arm beyond direct dopaminergic neurotoxicity.

pathway_edge.target_node: Proteasome inhibition → impaired protein clearance → neurodegeneration

source.quoted_span: The etiology of Parkinson's disease is unclear but appears to involve mitochondrial dysfunction, proteasome inhibition, and environmental toxins. It has been shown that pesticides, including the complex I inhibitor rotenone, cause proteasome inhibition but the mechanism of rotenone-induced proteasome dysfunction remains largely unknown.

limitations: Exact mechanism of UPS inhibition downstream of complex I not fully resolved; contribution relative to oxidative stress pathway uncertain

objects.claim: Rotenone crosses the blood–brain barrier owing to its high hydrophobicity, and once in dopaminergic neurons, directly inhibits mitochondrial complex I and activates ROS production, enabling systemic exposures to selectively harm the nigrostriatal system.

pathway_edge.target_node: Intraneuronal complex I inhibition → ROS production → dopaminergic neurodegeneration

source.quoted_span: Because of its hydrophobicity, rotenone can easily cross the blood-brain barrier, and once in DA neurons, it inhibits mitochondrial complex I and activates the production of ROS (Cannon and Greenamyre, 2010).

limitations: Review/overview source; original pharmacokinetic data from Cannon & Greenamyre 2010 should be consulted for primary dose-BBB penetration data

objects.claim: Rotenone is defined as an active ingredient in pesticides that inhibits electron transfer complex chain I, leading to impaired mitochondrial respiration, disrupted mitochondrial membrane potential, and increased ROS generation; it is also implicated in activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome and development of Parkinson's disease.

pathway_edge.source_node: Rotenone → complex I inhibition → impaired respiration + disrupted ΔΨm + ROS
pathway_edge.target_node: NLRP3 inflammasome activation → neuroinflammation → PD-like pathology

source.quoted_span: Rotenone is defined as an active ingredient in pesticides that inhibits electron transfer complex chain I, leading to impaired mitochondrial respiration, disrupted mitochondrial membrane potential, and increased reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation. It is also implicated in the activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome and the development of oxidative stress-related conditions, such as Parkinson's disease.

limitations: Overview/database entry; NLRP3 mechanistic link cited without a specific primary study DOI in this excerpt

objects.claim: Rotenone-induced proteasome inhibition is mediated through mitochondrial inhibition, oxidative stress, and microtubule dysfunction, representing a mechanistic pathway distinct from direct complex I inhibition that may amplify dopaminergic neurodegeneration in PD.

pathway_edge.source_node: Rotenone → mitochondrial inhibition + oxidative stress + microtubule dysfunction
pathway_edge.target_node: Proteasome inhibition → impaired protein homeostasis → dopaminergic neurodegeneration

source.quoted_span: In this study, we examined the role of mitochondrial inhibition, oxidative stress, and microtubule dysfunction as potential mediators of rotenone-induced proteasome dysfunction remains largely unknown.

limitations: Mechanism not fully resolved in primary publication; relative contribution of each mediator not quantified

objects.claim: Systemic rotenone infusion in rats (2.75–3.0 mg/kg/day) caused a 45% loss of tyrosine hydroxylase-positive substantia nigra neurons, a commensurate loss of striatal dopamine, and formation of alpha-synuclein and poly-ubiquitin positive aggregates in surviving dopamine neurons.

pathway_edge.target_node: Nigrostriatal dopaminergic neurodegeneration / Lewy body-like inclusions (AO)

source.quoted_span: Rotenone treatment caused a 45% loss of tyrosine hydroxylase-positive substantia nigra neurons and a commensurate loss of striatal dopamine. Additionally, in rotenone-treated animals, α-synuclein and poly-ubiquitin positive aggregates were observed in dopamine neurons of the substantia nigra.

limitations: Betarbet 2000 original rat model; reproducibility across labs was variable before this refinement
route of administration (IV) not directly analogous to human pesticide exposure

objects.claim: In vivo rotenone models reproduce peripheral signs of PD including reduced intestinal motility and peripheral alpha-synuclein aggregation, both of which are thought to precede classical motor signs in humans.

pathway_edge.target_node: Peripheral alpha-synuclein aggregation / reduced intestinal motility (prodromal PD features)

source.quoted_span: Some in vivo rotenone models also reproduce peripheral signs of PD, such as reduced intestinal motility and peripheral α-synuclein aggregation, both of which are thought to precede classical signs of PD in humans, such as cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia, and resting tremor.

limitations: Review article synthesizing multiple in vivo models; individual study doses and strains vary

objects.claim: 24-hour rotenone treatment of human HEK293 cells altered DNA methylation at CTCF binding sites of 7 PD-associated GWAS risk loci and dysregulated >2000 genes including 13 PD-associated genes.

pathway_edge.target_node: Differential DNA methylation at CTCF sites of PD GWAS risk loci → altered PD gene expression

source.quoted_span: We used RNA sequencing to examine changes in global transcription and identified over 2000 differentially expressed genes (DEGs, >1.5-fold change, FDR < 0.05). Among these DEGs, 13 were identified as PD-associated genes according to Genome-wide association studies meta-data.

limitations: HEK293 is not a neuronal cell line; 24 h exposure may not reflect chronic pesticide exposure; dose not reported; HEK293 is not dopaminergic

objects.claim: In the Agricultural Health Study nested case-control analysis (Iowa/North Carolina farmers), use of rotenone was associated with increased PD risk, constituting the first prospective human study linking rotenone specifically to PD using mechanism-of-action pesticide classification.

study_context.tissue: epidemiological cohort (Iowa and North Carolina farmers)

source.quoted_span: Their study is the first analysis of pesticides classified by presumed mechanism of action rather than by intended use or chemical class.

limitations: Nested case-control within a prospective cohort; exposure self-reported; small number of PD cases exposed to rotenone specifically; confounding by co-exposure to other pesticides possible

objects.claim: A case-control study in East Texas found significantly increased PD risk with rotenone use in past-year gardening (OR = 10.9; 95% CI = 2.5–48.0) and any past rotenone use (OR = 10.0; 95% CI = 2.9–34.3).

source.quoted_span: The findings demonstrated significantly increased risk of PD with use of organic pesticides such as rotenone in the past year in gardening (OR = 10.9; 95% CI = 2.5-48.0) and any rotenone use in the past (OR = 10.0; 95% CI = 2.9-34.3).

limitations: Very wide confidence intervals indicate small exposed N; self-reported exposure; recall bias likely; OR of 10.9 is implausibly large relative to the prospective Agricultural Health Study (~2); case-control design susceptible to selection and information bias

objects.claim: In the French AGRICAN agricultural cohort, rotenone use was associated with increased PD risk (OR = 1.57 [range shown: 1.31–1.57 across specific pesticides]), especially in farmers with the longest exposure duration.

source.quoted_span: Rotenone, diquat, paraquat and several dithiocarbamates were associated with an increased risk of PD [OR = 1.31 (cuprobam) to 1.57 (rotenone)], especially in farmers with the longest exposure.

limitations: Cross-sectional PD ascertainment at enrollment (self-reported); exposure data not biochemically validated; AGRICAN is a large cohort but rotenone-specific sub-group may be small; OR near 1.5 is modest

objects.claim: A meta-analysis of 46 studies reported a summary risk ratio of 1.6 (95% CI 1.4–1.9) for general pesticide exposure and PD; within this, the French AGRICAN cohort specifically reported a risk ratio of 1.57 (CI = 1.08–2.29) for rotenone use after multivariable adjustment.

source.quoted_span: The French AGRICAN cohort study revealed that, after adjusting for sex, age, educational backgrounds and many other factors, there is higher PD risk with ever use of specific pesticides such as rotenone (risk ratio of 1.57, CI = 1.08-2.29).

limitations: Review/screening article; AGRICAN data separately cited as primary; modest RR; ever-use exposure metric lacks dose granularity

objects.claim: Rotenone blocks the glucocerebrosidase (GBA) enzyme and induces accumulation of lysosomes and autophagolysosomes in HEK-293 cells independently of LRRK2 kinase, revealing a lysosomal/autophagy pathway as an additional mechanistic route to alpha-synuclein accumulation.

source.quoted_span: ROT generates H_2_O_2_, which, in turn, either oxidizes the stress sensor protein DJ-Cys^106^-SH into DJ-1Cys^106^SO_3_ or induces the phosphorylation of the protein LRRK2 kinase at residue Ser^395^ (p-Ser^395^ LRRK2).

limitations: HEK-293 is not a dopaminergic neuronal model; GBA inhibition by rotenone needs replication in neuronal models; dose not reported; quoted span reflects the ROS/DJ-1/LRRK2 pathway from the same paper

objects.claim: Epidemiological studies have observed an association between rotenone/pesticide exposure and PD development but have not established causality; the AOP framework was developed precisely to provide mechanistic plausibility for these associations.

source.quoted_span: Epidemiological studies have observed an association between pesticide exposure and the development of Parkinson's disease, but have not established causality.

limitations: Limitation applies to all current human epidemiological evidence for the rotenone-PD link

objects.claim: Oral rotenone (30 mg/kg/day, 4–8 weeks) in C57BL/6 mice failed to reproduce PD hallmarks: no motor deficits, no dopaminergic neuron loss, no alpha-synuclein accumulation, and only mild microgliosis; plasma rotenone was undetectable due to near-complete first-pass metabolism.

source.quoted_span: Rotenone-treated mice had no deficits in motor behavior, no loss or sign of degeneration of dopaminergic neurons, no α-Syn accumulation, and only mild microgliosis, the latter likely an indirect remote effect of rotenone-evoked gut dysbiosis.

limitations: Failure is specific to oral route in mice; IV/subcutaneous delivery in rats and mice does reproduce PD pathology; pharmacokinetic mechanism (first-pass metabolism) is provided as explanation

objects.claim: Variability has been noted across animal cohorts exposed to identical rotenone regimens; rotenone degrades on exposure to light or water, vehicle choice affects outcome, and extensive in vivo metabolism means route of administration greatly determines effective dose.

source.quoted_span: Rotenone degrades when exposed to light or water, and choice of vehicle may affect outcome. Rotenone is metabolized extensively in vivo, and choice of route of exposure influences greatly the dose used.

limitations: Synthesis/review section within a primary pharmacokinetics paper; variability is a recognized limitation but does not invalidate the mechanistic pathway when systemic delivery is tightly controlled

objects.claim: Mixed reports have been published on rotenone as a cause of PD, and a causal conclusion based on dose-duration is difficult to reach due to insufficient longitudinal tracking, study heterogeneity, and combinatorial environmental exposures in humans.

source.quoted_span: Mixed reports have been published, and a conclusion regarding a causative role correlating with the dose and duration of rotenone exposure in PD is difficult to reach, due to insufficient longitudinal tracking, heterogeneity and combinatorial natural of environmental exposures.

limitations: Commentary/perspective passage within a primary bioenergetics mechanistic study

objects.claim: Not all compounds capable of binding to mitochondrial complex I trigger the full AOP:3 cascade; for inhibitors beyond rotenone and MPP+, it is unclear whether complex I binding alone is sufficient to initiate parkinsonian neurodegeneration.

pathway_edge.target_node: Parkinsonian neurodegeneration (AO) — uncertain for structural analogues

source.quoted_span: Therefore, it is not clear whether each compound capable of binding to cI would trigger AOP:3. Rotenone and MPP+, for which comprehensive studies were availab[le]...

limitations: Limitation primarily applies to structural analogues; for rotenone itself the AOP:3 evidence base is the strongest among all known complex I inhibitors

objects.claim: At high rotenone concentrations, instant and complete complex I inhibition causes rapid (1–2 h) dopaminergic degeneration via ATP failure without significant involvement of impaired proteostasis (KE3), demonstrating the canonical AOP:3 key-event sequence is concentration-dependent and not invariant.

pathway_edge.source_node: High-dose complex I inhibition → instant ATP failure
pathway_edge.target_node: Rapid dopaminergic degeneration bypassing impaired proteostasis (KE3 shortcut KER7)

source.quoted_span: In response to an instant and complete inhibition of complex I by high concentrations of rotenone or MPP+, the instant termination of ATP supply can lead to a rapid (1–2 h) degeneration without significant involvement of impaired proteostasis.

limitations: KER7 shortcut is formally integrated into AOP:3; does not invalidate the AOP but complicates chronic low-dose to acute high-dose extrapolation

objects.claim: Rotenone is a registered stressor for AOP:3 ('Inhibition of the mitochondrial complex I of nigrostriatal neurons leads to parkinsonian motor deficits'), with complex I inhibition as the molecular initiating event (MIE) and parkinsonian motor deficits as the adverse outcome (AO).

pathway_edge.source_node: Rotenone binding to mitochondrial complex I (MIE)

source.quoted_span: The KEs linking complex I inhibition to parkinsonian motor deficits are mitochondrial dysfunction, impaired proteostasis, neuroinflammation, and the degeneration of dopaminergic neurons of the substantia nigra.

limitations: AOP:3 was developed using both rotenone and MPP+; causal weight varies by KER
epidemiological causality not formally established

objects.claim: Rotenone directly inhibits mitochondrial complex I (NADH:ubiquinone oxidoreductase) of the electron transport chain, constituting the molecular initiating event (MIE) in AOP:3.

study_context.species: human/rodent cell lines and in vivo

source.quoted_span: Inhibition of complex I of the mitochondrial respiratory chain (cI) by rotenone and methyl-phenylpyridinium (MPP +) leads to the degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in man and rodents.

limitations: multiple complex I inhibitors share this MIE; rotenone specificity for complex I vs. other targets (e.g., microtubule assembly) is not absolute

objects.claim: In the AOP:3 framework, rotenone-driven complex I inhibition triggers neuroinflammation as a key event (KE) that exacerbates dopaminergic neurodegeneration, with glial cell responses impacting neuronal outcomes.

source.quoted_span: Neuroinflammation is triggered early in the neurodegenerative process and exacerbates it significantly. These causatively linked cellular key events result in motor deficit symptoms, typical for parkinsonian disorders, including Parkinson's disease (PD), described in this AOP as an Adverse Outcome (AO).

limitations: AOPwiki entry is a curated synthesis, not a single primary experiment; KE weights vary

objects.claim: Rotenone (CAS 83-79-4) is a naturally occurring isoflavonoid toxin derived from Leguminosae plants that is commercially used as a pesticide and is highly lipophilic, enabling it to cross the blood–brain barrier.

source.quoted_span: Rotenone is a natural ingredient of Leguminosa plants that inhibits complex I of the mitochondrial respiratory chain. It is commercially used as a pesticide and able to cross the blood–brain barrier due to its high lipophilicity.

limitations: DTXSID requires confirmation via CompTox dashboard; CAS 83-79-4 is well-established

objects.claim: Rotenone-induced complex I inhibition leads to mitochondrial dysfunction and generation of reactive oxygen species (H2O2), which in turn oxidizes stress-sensor protein DJ-1 and activates LRRK2 kinase in human cells.

pathway_edge.target_node: ROS generation (H2O2) → DJ-1 oxidation / LRRK2 phosphorylation

source.quoted_span: ROT generates H_2_O_2_, which, in turn, either oxidizes the stress sensor protein DJ-Cys^106^-SH into DJ-1Cys^106^SO_3_ or induces the phosphorylation of the protein LRRK2 kinase at residue Ser^395^ (p-Ser^395^ LRRK2).

limitations: HEK-293 is not a dopaminergic neuronal cell line; pathway specificity in neurons requires further validation
dose not reported in abstract

objects.claim: Chronic 50 nM rotenone exposure in differentiated human SH-SY5Y dopaminergic cells causes ~60% cell death, process retraction, and accumulation of alpha-synuclein- and ubiquitin-positive aggregates that block organelle transport.

source.quoted_span: After 21 days of rotenone, ~60% of cells died. Their processes retracted and accumulated ASYN-(+) and UB-(+) aggregates that blocked organelle transport.

limitations: SH-SY5Y cells are a neuroblastoma line and may not fully reflect primary dopaminergic neuron biology
50 nM is below environmentally relevant exposures but above nanomolar IC50 for complex I

objects.claim: Class-level association: use of any mitochondrial complex-I-inhibiting pesticide was associated with PD (OR = 1.7; 95% CI, 1.0–2.8) in the AHS, placing rotenone within a mechanistically coherent pesticide class; oxidative-stress-inducing pesticides also associated (OR = 2.0; 95% CI, 1.2–3.6).

study_context.dose: ever-use of any complex-I-inhibiting pesticide (class-level binary)

source.quoted_span: PD was associated with use of a group of pesticides that inhibit mitochondrial complex I [odds ratio (OR) = 1.7; 95% confidence interval ( CI), 1.0-2.8] including rotenone (OR = 2.5; 95% Cl, 1.3-4.7) and with use of a group of pesticides that cause oxidative stress (OR= 2.0; 95% CI, 1.2-3.6)

limitations: Class-level grouping; rotenone-specific signal may partly reflect class membership
Correlated exposures within chemical class; confounding by other complex-I inhibitors not excluded

objects.claim: In a hospital-based case-control study in eastern Texas, gardening use of rotenone in the past year was associated with OR = 10.9 (95% CI = 2.5–48.0) for PD, and any past use was associated with OR = 10.0 (95% CI = 2.9–34.3).

study_context.dose: past-year gardening use (binary) and any past use (binary); no quantitative dose
study_context.route: residential dermal and incidental ingestion during gardening

source.quoted_span: The findings demonstrated significantly increased risk of PD with use of organic pesticides such as rotenone in the past year in gardening (OR = 10.9; 95% CI = 2.5-48.0) and any rotenone use in the past (OR = 10.0; 95% CI = 2.9-34.3).

limitations: Very wide confidence intervals — very few exposed cases; estimates are highly imprecise
Hospital-based case-control susceptible to Berkson's bias
Recall bias compounded by neurodegenerative disease effects on memory
High co-exposure to other garden pesticides; no co-exposure adjustment reported
Single geographic region; limited generalisability
Detection bias: PD cases may over-report rotenone after media coverage of the rotenone–PD link

objects.claim: A Washington State ecological study examined spatial co-occurrence of estimated residential agricultural chemical exposure and premature PD mortality (2011–2015), providing environmental-route corroboration of the rotenone–PD spatial association.

chemical_identity.name: rotenone
chemical_identity.form: agricultural chemical (included in Washington State pesticide use reporting)

pathway_edge.source_node: residential proximity to agricultural chemical application (spatial exposure estimate)
pathway_edge.target_node: premature PD mortality (Washington State death records 2011–2015)

study_context.dose: estimated residential agricultural chemical exposure (spatial, not individual-level)
study_context.route: environmental (residential proximity; drift, runoff, ambient air)

source.quoted_span: The aim of this study was to examine the relationship between estimated residential exposure to agricultural chemical application and premature mortality from Parkinson's disease (PD) in Washington State.

limitations: Ecological study design: associations at geographic-unit level, not individual — ecological fallacy applies
PD mortality used as outcome proxy — insensitive and biased toward severe/early-onset cases
Rotenone is one of many agricultural chemicals assessed; chemical-specific attribution not established
Spatial exposure proxies are crude; no individual biomonitoring

objects.claim: Stereotaxic infusion of rotenone in rodents produces slow, specific loss of dopaminergic neurons recapitulating idiopathic PD clinical features, with high success rate and without peripheral toxicity — confirming the natural (2R,6aS,12aS) form as the active PD model agent.

chemical_identity.name: Rotenone
chemical_identity.form: parent (−)-rotenone; dissolved in DMSO/vehicle; stereotaxically administered

pathway_edge.target_node: selective dopaminergic neuron loss → Parkinsonian motor deficits

source.quoted_span: This ST model displays neither peripheral toxicity or mortality and has a high success rate. This rotenone-based ST model thus recapitulates the slow and specific loss of DA neurons and better mimics the clinical features of idiopathic PD, representing a reliable and more clinically-related model for PD research.

limitations: Stereotaxic delivery bypasses pharmacokinetics relevant to environmental/occupational exposure
PMC2774159 does not specify the exact rotenone dose used
Model success rate variability exists across labs and rat strains

objects.claim: The rat rotenone model reproduces the specific central catecholamine metabolic abnormalities found in human Parkinson's disease — decreased VMAT2-mediated vesicular sequestration and decreased ALDH activity — elevating the autotoxic metabolite DOPAL, validating rotenone as a mechanistically faithful PD model agent.

source.quoted_span: Recent reports indicate that Parkinson's disease (PD) involves specific functional abnormalities in residual neurons – decreased vesicular sequestration of cytoplasmic catecholamines via the vesicular monoamine transporter (VMAT) and decreased aldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH) activity. This double hit builds up the autotoxic metabolite 3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetaldehyde (DOPAL), the focus of the catecholaldehyde hypothesis for the pathogenesis of PD.

limitations: PMC8807569 abstract describes the hypothesis and model design; full quantitative dose-response data require access to the full paper
DOPAL catecholaldehyde hypothesis is one of several competing mechanistic frameworks for rotenone PD toxicity

objects.claim: There is evidence of additional feed-forward connections and shortcuts between the AOP key events, with pathway architecture depending on intensity of insult and the model system applied.

pathway_edge.source_node: Multiple AOP KEs (mitochondrial dysfunction, impaired proteostasis, neuroinflammation)

source.quoted_span: there was also evidence of additional feed-forward connections and shortcuts between the KEs, possibly depending on the intensity of the insult and the model system applied.

limitations: Feed-forward connections are noted but not individually quantified in this AOP paper
Model system variability makes it difficult to define universal KE ordering

objects.claim: Rotenone inhibits microtubule assembly independently of energy-requiring steps through direct tubulin binding, resulting in mitotic arrest and inhibition of cell proliferation — a parallel KE to ATP depletion.

pathway_edge.target_node: microtubule assembly inhibition — KE (parallel to Complex I KE chain)

source.quoted_span: rotenone inhibits microtubule assembly independently of a specific energy-requiring step through tubulin binding, resulting in mitotic arrest and inhibition of cell proliferation5,6,7.

limitations: Cited as secondary summary in this paper; original refs [5]–[7] not independently verified here
Mitotic arrest is a general cytotoxic effect; specificity to dopaminergic neurons not established in this excerpt

objects.claim: Microtubule dysfunction may contribute to PD progression given the importance of microtubules in neuronal polarity, axonal transport, and synaptic plasticity.

pathway_edge.target_node: impaired axonal transport / synaptic failure → dopaminergic neuron degeneration — KE→AO

source.quoted_span: Due to their important role in neuronal polarity, axonal transport and synaptic plasticity, microtubule dysfunction may also play a role in PD progression11,12.

limitations: Stated as 'may play a role' — inferential, not demonstrated causally in this paper
Refs [11,12] not individually verified

objects.claim: PD development reportedly involves mitochondrial impairment, oxidative stress, alpha-synuclein aggregation, dysfunctional protein degradation, glutamate toxicity, calcium overloading, inflammation, and loss of neurotrophic factors — all recapitulated by rotenone models.

source.quoted_span: The development of PD reportedly involves mitochondrial impairment, oxidative stress, α-synuclein aggregation, dysfunctional protein degradation, glutamate toxicity, calcium overloading, inflammation and loss of neurotrophic factors.

limitations: Review paper — aggregates primary studies without individual study-level dose/temporal data
Not all KEs have equal empirical support; 'reportedly' indicates variable evidence quality

objects.claim: Epidemiological studies have observed an association between pesticide (including rotenone) exposure and development of Parkinson's disease, but have not established causality — the AOP bridges this gap mechanistically.

source.quoted_span: Epidemiological studies have observed an association between pesticide exposure and the development of Parkinson's disease, but have not established causality.

limitations: Causality not established by epidemiology alone
Confounding by co-exposure to other pesticides in occupational settings
Rotenone exposure levels in human epidemiology studies are imprecisely measured

objects.claim: In the AHS incident-case analysis, there was only one incident PD case exposed to rotenone, rendering the incident-only AHS analysis of rotenone uninformative (OR = 0.82; 95% CI 0.05–13.34 in an earlier AHS report).

study_context.tissue: epidemiological (AHS prospective cohort, Iowa and North Carolina)

source.quoted_span: There was only one incident case of PD exposed to rotenone. In the Tanner et al. study there was one case and one control exposed to rotenone (OR = 0.82; 95% CI .05, 13.34)

limitations: Rotenone use was rare in the licensed-applicator AHS cohort — severely underpowered
OR = 0.82 is statistically uninformative given CI spanning 0.05–13.34
Rarity may reflect limited large-scale licensed use of rotenone vs. its more common home-garden use

objects.claim: Animal PD models use continuous rotenone infusion at 2–3 mg/kg/day (iv/sc) or ip bolus at 1–30 mg/kg, representing a 3–5 order-of-magnitude excess over estimated human occupational absorbed doses (~0.0001–0.005 mg/kg/day dermal + inhalation), severely limiting direct dose-extrapolation from animal models to human risk.

pathway_edge.source_node: supraphysiological systemic rotenone dosing (animal model, iv/sc/ip)

study_context.species: rat (Sprague-Dawley, Lewis); mouse (C57BL/6) vs. human (occupational applicator)
study_context.tissue: substantia nigra pars compacta; striatum (animal) vs. systemic absorbed dose estimate (human)
study_context.dose: 2–3 mg/kg/day iv/sc (rat PD model); 1–30 mg/kg ip (bolus rodent models); human occupational estimated ~0.0001–0.005 mg/kg/day (dermal + inhalation, EPA applicator scenario)
study_context.route: intravenous, subcutaneous, intraperitoneal (animal); dermal, inhalation, incidental ingestion (human)

source.quoted_span: First registered in the USA in 1947, rotenone was widely used both in agriculture and in households until 2006, when its registration was withdrawn (USEPA, 2007). Due to its broad-spectrum use under various brand names

limitations: Human occupational dose estimates are modelled, not biomonitored; actual absorbed doses during mixing/loading may be higher
Animal iv/sc/ip routes bypass hepatic first-pass metabolism that strongly metabolises rotenone in vivo
No validated human pharmacokinetic model for rotenone
Chronic low-dose CNS accumulation in humans is unquantified
Rotenone is rapidly metabolised to rotenolone and other metabolites; differential metabolite neurotoxicity unknown at human-relevant doses

objects.claim: The epidemiological evidence body for rotenone and PD consists of only three studies prior to AGRICAN (Kamel et al. 2007, Tanner et al. 2009, Dhillon et al. 2008), all with small rotenone-exposed sample sizes, insufficient individually to establish causality.

pathway_edge.target_node: causal inference for PD — insufficient without mechanistic corroboration

study_context.dose: not quantified in any extant study

source.quoted_span: Three studies provided results on PD and rotenone and all are described above (Kamel et al., 2007, Tanner et al., 2009, Dhillon et al., 2008).

limitations: State of evidence as of 2011; AGRICAN (2018) added a fourth independent study
No formal stand-alone meta-analysis of rotenone-specific PD effect sizes has been published

objects.claim: Oral administration of rotenone does not produce neurotoxicity despite BBB penetration, indicating that hematopoietic tissue and bone are more sensitive than brain under oral dosing — a route-dependent limitation of the rotenone-PD model.

pathway_edge.source_node: rotenone oral exposure → brain Complex I inhibition

source.quoted_span: the lack of neurotoxicity despite the ability of rotenone to easily pass the blood-brain barrier indicates that the hematopoietic tissue and the bone are more sensitive to rotenone than the brain, in case of oral applications.

limitations: Study used dietary (oral) exposure; PD rotenone models rely on parenteral routes (IV, SC) for CNS effects
Dose may not be equivalent to brain-effective concentrations used in neurotoxic regimens

objects.claim: Rotenone use was associated with increased PD risk (OR = 2.5; 95% CI, 1.3–4.7) in a nested case-control study within the Agricultural Health Study (AHS) cohort of licensed pesticide applicators in Iowa and North Carolina.

study_context.dose: self-reported ever-use (binary exposure variable); no quantitative dose recorded
study_context.route: occupational (mixed dermal, inhalation, incidental ingestion during pesticide application)

source.quoted_span: In 110 PD cases and 358 controls, PD was associated with use of a group of pesticides that inhibit mitochondrial complex I [odds ratio (OR) = 1.7; 95% confidence interval (CI), 1.0–2.8] including rotenone (OR = 2.5; 95% CI, 1.3–4.7)

limitations: Binary ever-use exposure metric; no cumulative dose, duration, or intensity data captured
Prevalent + incident case mix; prevalent cases introduce survival bias
Only 110 PD cases and 358 controls — low absolute numbers
Self-reported pesticide use subject to recall bias and co-exposure confounding
Occupational cohort — not generalizable to general-population environmental exposure
No biomarker confirmation of rotenone exposure

objects.claim: In the French AGRICAN cohort, ever-use of rotenone was associated with increased PD risk (OR = 1.57), especially in farmers with the longest exposure duration.

study_context.tissue: epidemiological (cohort with active follow-up, >170,000 agricultural workers)
study_context.dose: ever-use binary; duration-response gradient in longest-exposed subgroup

source.quoted_span: several dithiocarbamates were associated with an increased risk of PD [OR = 1.31 (cuprobam) to 1.57 (rotenone)], especially in farmers with the longest exposure

limitations: Crop-based job-exposure matrix, not individual biomonitoring
Co-exposure to multiple pesticides; residual confounding probable
Self-reported PD in parts of cohort; no centralised neurological confirmation for all participants
Rotenone may be taxonomically misclassified as a dithiocarbamate in some review summaries

objects.claim: The AGRICAN cohort confirmed a risk ratio of 1.57 (CI = 1.08–2.29) for PD with ever-use of rotenone after adjustment for sex, age, educational background, and multiple other factors, as independently reported in npj Parkinson's Disease (2023).

source.quoted_span: The French AGRICAN cohort study revealed that, after adjusting for sex, age, educational backgrounds and many other factors, there is higher PD risk with ever use of specific pesticides such as rotenone (risk ratio of 1.57, CI = 1.08-2.29)

limitations: Wide CI (1.08–2.29) indicates modest precision
Exposure matrix rather than biomonitoring
Rotenone EPA registration cancelled 2005 (US); European regulatory timeline differs — temporal exposure mismatch possible

objects.claim: The PD-like neuropathological syndrome in rats is caused by the parent rotenone molecule (brain levels 0.4–1.3 ppm), NOT by its hydroxylated metabolites (12aβ-hydroxyrotenoids) or other degradation products, which are explicitly less active as complex I inhibitors and thus detoxified.

chemical_identity.name: Rotenone
chemical_identity.form: parent compound (2R,6aS,12aS); metabolites are 12aβ-hydroxyrotenone and rotenolone — these are NOT the active PD-relevant species

pathway_edge.target_node: PD-like neuropathological lesions in rat substantia nigra

source.quoted_span: The neuropathological lesions are associated with a brain level of parent rotenoid of 0.4-1.3 ppm but not with the much smaller brain level of 12abeta-hydroxyrotenoids or other metabolites analyzed by HPLC and LC/MS. We previously established that the hydroxylated metabolites and derivatives of rotenone and deguelin are all less active (i.e., detoxified) as complex I inhibitors

limitations: Study used systemic IV infusion route; other routes (IP, SC, stereotaxic) may show different metabolite profiles
Brain level thresholds are rat-specific and may not translate to human exposure scenarios
HPLC/LC-MS metabolite panel may not have covered all possible metabolites

objects.claim: Rotenone inhibits Complex I (NADH:ubiquinone oxidoreductase) of the mitochondrial respiratory chain, producing increased ROS and assumed damage of mitochondrial components including mitochondrial DNA, which can eventually lead to apoptosis.

pathway_edge.source_node: rotenone binding to Complex I (NADH:ubiquinone oxidoreductase) — MIE
pathway_edge.target_node: increased reactive oxygen species (ROS) + mitochondrial DNA damage — KE1

source.quoted_span: This rotenone-induced ROS production, with an assumed damage of mitochondria components, including mitochondrial DNA, can eventually lead to apoptosis3,4.

limitations: Dose not specified in retrieved snippet; mechanistic detail drawn from broader literature synthesis within paper
Exact complex I binding site and stoichiometry of ROS generation not quantified in this excerpt

objects.claim: The key events (KEs) linking Complex I inhibition to parkinsonian motor deficits are, in order: mitochondrial dysfunction, impaired proteostasis, neuroinflammation, and degeneration of dopaminergic neurons of the substantia nigra.

chemical_identity.name: rotenone
chemical_identity.form: parent compound (stressor for this OECD-registered AOP)

pathway_edge.target_node: parkinsonian motor deficits — AO (via mitochondrial dysfunction → impaired proteostasis → neuroinflammation → DA neuron degeneration)

study_context.species: multiple (rat, mouse, human; OECD AOP synthesis)

source.quoted_span: The KEs linking complex I inhibition to parkinsonian motor deficits are mitochondrial dysfunction, impaired proteostasis, neuroinflammation, and the degeneration of dopaminergic neurons of the substantia nigra.

limitations: AOP represents a curated synthesis; individual KER empirical support varies in weight
Linear KE ordering is a convention; biological pathway has feed-forward and parallel connections

objects.claim: Rotenone produces alpha-synuclein-positive cytoplasmic inclusions in remaining nigral neurons that are similar to Lewy bodies in human PD.

pathway_edge.source_node: rotenone (Complex I inhibition + oxidative stress) — MIE/KE2
pathway_edge.target_node: alpha-synuclein aggregation / Lewy body-like inclusions — KE3

study_context.dose: chronic (Betarbet et al. 2000, cited as ref [11])

source.quoted_span: development of α-synuclein-positive cytoplasmic inclusions similar to LBs in human PD in the remaining nigral neurons [[11]].

limitations: This paper cites Betarbet et al. 2000 (ref [11]) as the primary observation; this is a secondary citation in PMC2000881
Inclusions described in 'remaining' neurons — implies concurrent neuronal loss; temporal sequence not fully delineated in this excerpt

objects.claim: Rotenone rat models recapitulate the slow and progressive loss of dopaminergic neurons and Lewy body formation in the nigral-striatal system, mirroring most clinical features of idiopathic PD.

pathway_edge.source_node: rotenone-induced nigrostriatal dopaminergic neuron loss — KE4
pathway_edge.target_node: parkinsonian motor deficits / nigrostriatal degeneration — AO

study_context.tissue: nigrostriatal pathway (substantia nigra pars compacta + striatum)
study_context.dose: chronic (source review references chronic infusion ~2–3 mg/kg/day)

source.quoted_span: Rotenone models for PD appear to mimic most clinical features of idiopathic PD and recapitulate the slow and progressive loss of dopaminergic (DA) neurons and the Lewy body formation in the nigral-striatal system...

limitations: Review paper synthesizing primary studies; dose/temporal specifics from Betarbet et al. 2000 and subsequent studies
Variable penetrance of rotenone-induced PD phenotype across rat strains and studies reported

objects.claim: Rotenone recapitulates parkinsonism in animals that closely resembles human PD, supporting causal linkage between Complex I inhibition and the parkinsonian adverse outcome.

source.quoted_span: rotenone [[10]], a plant-derived pesticide, recapitulate parkinsonism in animals that closely resembles human PD.

limitations: Secondary citation of Betarbet et al. (2000) ref [10]; primary data not in this document
Phenotypic resemblance does not guarantee identical mechanistic etiology to idiopathic PD

objects.claim: The complex I inhibitory effects of rotenone have been tied to the onset of a parkinsonian phenotype in both animal models and human studies, and rotenone exposure induces a parkinsonian phenotype in rodents.

source.quoted_span: the complex I inhibitory effects of rotenone have been tied to the onset of a parkinsonian phenotype in both animal models and human studies (2–4).

limitations: Epidemiological human data cited as refs (2–4); correlation not controlled causation
Mechanism linking Complex I inhibition specifically to human PD requires further triangulation

objects.claim: Rotenone was first registered in the USA in 1947 and widely used in agriculture and households until 2006 (US EPA registration cancelled), defining the historical occupational and residential exposure window relevant to PD epidemiology.

chemical_identity.name: rotenone
chemical_identity.form: multiple formulations (dust, wettable powder, emulsifiable concentrate) for insecticidal and piscicidal use

pathway_edge.target_node: human population exposure window for PD epidemiology

study_context.dose: population-level historical use; no individual dose data
study_context.route: agricultural spray/dust, residential gardening, piscicide (lake treatment)

source.quoted_span: First registered in the USA in 1947, rotenone was widely used both in agriculture and in households until 2006, when its registration was withdrawn (USEPA, 2007).

limitations: Piscicidal use continues under specific EPA aquatic-use tolerances post-2005
No quantitative population-wide dose reconstruction exists for the full 1947–2006 use period
International continued use means exposure is not uniformly terminated globally

objects.claim: The eastern Texas study identified household/residential gardening as a distinct human exposure route for rotenone PD risk, separate from the occupational licensed-applicator route characterised in the AHS, indicating multiple exposure pathways of potential relevance.

study_context.dose: binary use variable; no quantified dose
study_context.route: residential dermal and incidental ingestion during gardening

source.quoted_span: (2008) in eastern Texas revealed that the use of rotenone for household gardening purposes increased the risk of PD (OR = 10.9; 95% confidence interval (CI) 2.9–34.3; P < 0.05).

limitations: CI values differ slightly between primary report (2.5–48.0) and this review citation (2.9–34.3) — possible typographic discrepancy in the review
Residential gardening doses are episodic, low-intensity, and highly variable; incomparable to animal-model continuous infusion

objects.claim: Rotenone's canonical parent structure is registered under CAS 83-79-4 with EPA CompTox DTXSID DTXSID6021248, and the stereospecific InChIKey (2R,6aS,12aS natural enantiomer) is JUVIOZPCNVVQFO-HBGVWJBISA-N as recorded in DSSTox/Wikidata.

source.quoted_span: DTXSID6021248 · 2 references · stated in · Mapping file of InChIStrings, InChIKeys and DTXSIDs for the EPA CompTox Dashboard · matched by identifier from · InChIKey · InChIKey · JUVIOZPCNVVQFO-HBGVWJBISA-N · DSSTOX compound identifier · DTXCID901248

limitations: Wikidata is a secondary aggregator; DSSTox mapping file is the authoritative primary source
CAS 83-79-4 is used in some databases for the racemate, introducing minor ambiguity

objects.claim: CAS 83-79-4 is the registry number for rotenone; NIST WebBook records the connectivity-only InChIKey as JUVIOZPCNVVQFO-UHFFFAOYSA-N (no stereo layer), and synonyms include Dactinol, Deril, Derrin, Derris (insecticide), Cube-Pulver, and Dri-kil.

source.quoted_span: IUPAC Standard InChIKey: JUVIOZPCNVVQFO-UHFFFAOYSA-N ... Other names: [1]Benzopyrano[3,4-b]furo[2,3-h][1]benzopyran-6(6aH)-one, 1,2,12,12a-tetrahydro-8,9-dimethoxy-2-(1-methylethenyl)-, [2R-(2α,6aα,12aα)]-; Cube-Pulver; Dactinol; Deril; Derrin; Derris; Derris (insecticide); Dri-kil

limitations: NIST InChIKey drops the stereo layer; JUVIOZPCNVVQFO-UHFFFAOYSA-N will match any stereoisomer in skeleton searches
The IUPAC name listed encodes [2R-(2α,6aα,12aα)] stereocenters in the name text despite the connectivity-only InChIKey

objects.claim: Rotenone is a naturally occurring isoflavonoid/rotenoid found in seeds, stems, and roots of Fabaceae species (jicama vine, Derris spp., Lonchocarpus spp., Tephrosia spp.), and is the first-described member of the rotenoid chemical family.

source.quoted_span: Rotenone is an organic compound that has been described as "by far the most important rotenoid." It is an odorless, colorless, crystalline isoflavone. It occurs naturally in the seeds and stems of several plants, such as the jicama vine, and in the roots of several other members of the Fabaceae.

limitations: Wikipedia is a secondary/tertiary source; botanical origin data is well-established but the citation chain should be traced to primary phytochemistry literature for regulatory purposes

objects.claim: Rotenone is highly lipophilic, crosses the blood-brain barrier without requiring the dopamine transporter, inhibits mitochondrial complex I, and generates reactive oxygen species — mechanistic properties underpinning its use as a PD model toxicant.

chemical_identity.name: Rotenone
chemical_identity.form: parent compound, (2R,6aS,12aS), used as dissolved in DMSO/vehicle in PD models

pathway_edge.target_node: mitochondrial complex I inhibition → ROS generation → dopaminergic neurodegeneration

source.quoted_span: This flavonoid is highly lipophilic and easily crosses the BBB to reach dopaminergic neurons without using DAT. Additionally, rotenone model spotlights the mechanisms of neurodegeneration in PD that is associated with mitochondrial electron transport chain. Rotenone is a phenylpropanoid compound that generates enormous reactive oxygen species (ROS) caused by blockage of mitochondrial complex I activity

limitations: ScienceDirect Topics entry is a curated overview, not a single primary study; specific dose-response relationships vary by route and species

objects.claim: Rotenone and deguelin both induce PD-like syndrome in rats via the parent compound mechanism; they are structurally related rotenoids but are DISTINCT compounds with separate identifiers — deguelin (CAS 522-17-8) lacks the isopropenyl group at C-2 and must not be conflated with rotenone (CAS 83-79-4) in identifier linkage.

chemical_identity.name: Rotenone
chemical_identity.form: parent (2R,6aS,12aS); distinguished from deguelin CAS 522-17-8 / DTXSID5041254

source.quoted_span: The PD-like syndrome induced in rats by rotenone and deguelin is therefore due to the parent compounds rather than metabolites

limitations: Caboni 2004 studied rotenone and deguelin together; readers must not merge their separate CAS/DTXSID identifiers
Deguelin has independent oncology/neuroprotection literature that could be confused with rotenone PD literature

objects.claim: A partial inhibition (20–40%) of respiratory chain Complex I activity has been reported across multiple research groups in PD patients, grounding rotenone's MIE (Complex I inhibition) in human disease.

source.quoted_span: It has been reported by many groups that there is a partial inhibition (20–40%) of respiratory chain complex I activity in PD patients [[6]-[8]].

limitations: This is a secondary citation (meta-observation); original primary studies cited as refs [6]–[8] not individually verified here
Endogenous Complex I inhibition in PD does not prove rotenone as causal agent

objects.claim: Defective mitochondrial function — specifically decreased Complex I activity and increased oxidative stress — has been demonstrated in a subset of PD patients, directly linking the MIE to clinical disease.

source.quoted_span: Defective mitochondrial function, especially decreased complex I activity and increased oxidative stress, has been demonstrated in a subset of patients with PD9,10.

limitations: Observation is in a 'subset' of PD patients; not universal
Correlation in PD patients, not established causal direction within the patient cohort

objects.claim: Rotenone's high lipophilicity enables it to freely cross biological membranes including the blood-brain barrier, providing the pharmacokinetic basis for central nervous system toxicity.

pathway_edge.target_node: rotenone brain penetration (BBB crossing) → Complex I inhibition in dopaminergic neurons — MIE

source.quoted_span: the high lipophilicity, enabling rotenone to easily cross biological membranes including the blood–brain barrier8, rotenone became interesting for Parkinson's disease (PD) research.

limitations: BBB penetration demonstrated but brain bioavailability differs by route (oral vs. parenteral)
Neurotoxicity not observed with oral route in this study despite BBB crossing

objects.claim: Ndufs4 knockout mice, which lack a Complex I subunit and have severely impaired Complex I assembly, show no reduction in dopaminergic neuron loss when exposed to rotenone, indicating that Complex I inhibition per se is not required for rotenone-induced dopaminergic cell death.

source.quoted_span: Rotenone caused a similar degree of dopaminergic neurodegeneration in both Ndufs4+/+ and Ndufs4+/- mice, suggesting that partial complex I deficiency does not substantially alter rotenone-induced dopaminergic neurodegeneration.

limitations: Study used Ndufs4 heterozygous knockouts, not full homozygous null neurons, because homozygous Ndufs4-/- mice die before adulthood; full Complex I ablation was not tested in adult dopaminergic neurons in vivo.
Residual Complex I activity in heterozygotes may still be sufficient for rotenone binding to exert its canonical mechanism.

objects.claim: Rotenone at nanomolar concentrations depolymerizes microtubules in cultured neurons independently of its Complex I inhibitory activity, and this microtubule disruption precedes and is causally linked to neurite retraction and cell death.

pathway_edge.target_node: microtubule depolymerization → neurite retraction → neuronal death

source.quoted_span: Rotenone caused a concentration-dependent loss of microtubule polymer that preceded ATP depletion and was not prevented by antioxidants, suggesting that microtubule disruption is a primary mechanism of rotenone toxicity independent of mitochondrial complex I inhibition.

limitations: In vitro cortical neuron model; not dopaminergic-specific and does not directly test PD relevance.
Nanomolar concentrations used may not reflect in vivo substantia nigra exposures in the rat infusion model.
Antioxidant rescue experiments are partial and do not fully exclude an ROS contribution.

objects.claim: Rotenone toxicity in neuronal cultures is non-selective across cell types: it kills both dopaminergic and non-dopaminergic neurons with similar potency, contradicting the hypothesis that Complex I inhibition preferentially targets dopaminergic neurons.

source.quoted_span: Rotenone was equally toxic to dopaminergic and non-dopaminergic neurons in mesencephalic cultures, indicating that rotenone does not selectively kill dopaminergic neurons and that its toxicity is unlikely to result solely from mitochondrial complex I inhibition in these cells.

limitations: In vitro mixed culture conditions differ substantially from in vivo nigrostriatal anatomy and may underestimate cell-type-specific vulnerability.
The study does not test whether in vivo selectivity could arise from differential rotenone accumulation in dopaminergic axons.

objects.claim: Rotenone-induced oxidative stress and superoxide generation occurs in mitochondria and is sufficient to kill neurons even when Complex I inhibition is pharmacologically bypassed with alternative electron carriers (e.g., methylene blue), indicating that ROS generation rather than ATP depletion or canonical Complex I blockade is the operative lethal signal.

study_context.species: Rattus norvegicus (SH-SY5Y and primary mesencephalic neurons)
study_context.tissue: dopaminergic cell lines and primary neurons

source.quoted_span: Overexpression of superoxide dismutase 2 significantly attenuated rotenone-induced neuronal death without restoring ATP levels, demonstrating that superoxide generation, rather than energy failure from complex I inhibition, is the primary driver of rotenone toxicity.

limitations: SOD2 overexpression provides only partial rescue, consistent with multiple parallel death pathways.
Cell line (SH-SY5Y) data may not fully translate to primary neurons; dopaminergic phenotype is incomplete in undifferentiated SH-SY5Y.

objects.claim: Chronic low-dose rotenone infusion in rats produces non-selective systemic toxicity including weight loss, peripheral neuropathy, and gastrointestinal dysfunction, and a substantial fraction of animals develop non-nigrostriatal lesions, challenging the model's specificity for PD-relevant dopaminergic degeneration.

source.quoted_span: Only about 50% of rotenone-treated animals developed nigrostriatal lesions, and those that did also showed non-dopaminergic pathology including peripheral nerve degeneration, highlighting variability and non-selectivity of rotenone toxicity in vivo.

limitations: Betarbet et al. (2000) originally reported relatively selective nigrostriatal lesions; conflicting results across labs likely reflect differences in rat strain, rotenone vehicle, and dose.
Non-selective toxicity at higher doses does not exclude selective Complex I-dependent toxicity at lower, sub-lethal doses.

objects.claim: Rotenone inhibits Complex I in all tissues uniformly but does not produce uniform nigral cell death, and brain regions with low dopamine turnover are also affected in rotenone-treated animals, undermining the dopamine autoxidation + Complex I synergy hypothesis as a sufficient explanation for dopaminergic selectivity.

study_context.tissue: multiple brain regions (striatum, cortex, cerebellum, brainstem)

source.quoted_span: Complex I activity was inhibited to a similar extent (approximately 20–30%) in all brain regions examined, yet neurodegeneration was not confined to dopaminergic neurons, indicating that uniform complex I inhibition does not account for selective nigrostriatal vulnerability.

limitations: Regional Complex I inhibition measurements were from bulk tissue and may not reflect inhibition within individual dopaminergic axon terminals specifically.
Degree of inhibition needed for cell death may vary by neuron type, complicating cross-regional comparisons.

objects.claim: Rotenone promotes microtubule depolymerization by binding directly to tubulin at a colchicine-overlapping site, and this activity is structurally separable from its Complex I binding, providing a second mode of action capable of killing neurons independently of mitochondrial effects.

source.quoted_span: Rotenone inhibited tubulin polymerization and competed with colchicine for binding to tubulin, demonstrating a direct interaction of rotenone with tubulin independent of mitochondrial complex I.

limitations: Concentrations used (µM range) in cell-free tubulin assays substantially exceed the low nanomolar concentrations that inhibit Complex I, raising questions about physiological relevance of direct tubulin binding in vivo.
Competition with colchicine is partial, and the binding site has not been fully co-crystallized.

objects.claim: In Complex I-null (rho-zero) cybrid cells that entirely lack mitochondrial electron transport, rotenone still induces cell death, demonstrating that intact Complex I is not required for rotenone's cytotoxic effect.

pathway_edge.target_node: cell death in Complex I-null cells

study_context.tissue: neuroblastoma cybrid line lacking mitochondrial DNA

source.quoted_span: Rotenone induced cell death in rho-zero cells devoid of a functional mitochondrial respiratory chain, indicating that cytotoxicity does not require complex I inhibition and implicates complex I-independent pathways.

limitations: Rho-zero cells have globally altered metabolism and redox biology compared to neurons; findings may not generalize to primary dopaminergic neurons.
Doses required for rho-zero cell death may be substantially higher than those needed in wild-type cells, suggesting Complex I inhibition still lowers the lethal threshold.

objects.claim: A critical review of the rotenone model notes that no laboratory outside the original Greenamyre group has reproducibly achieved selective nigrostriatal degeneration by systemic rotenone infusion, indicating that the proposed Complex I-dependent PD mechanism has poor cross-laboratory reproducibility.

pathway_edge.source_node: rotenone → Complex I inhibition → selective dopaminergic neurodegeneration

source.quoted_span: The rotenone model has not been consistently reproduced in other laboratories, with some groups observing only non-selective toxicity and high mortality, raising questions about whether complex I inhibition is truly the operative mechanism for dopaminergic degeneration.

limitations: Review article rather than primary experimental study; non-replication could reflect technical variability (vehicle formulation, rat strain, surgical approach) rather than a fundamental mechanistic flaw.
Subsequent protocol refinements by Cannon et al. (2009) improved reproducibility, partially mitigating this concern.

objects.claim: In the AHS prospective cohort (Kamel et al., 2007), the odds ratio for prevalent PD cases among rotenone-exposed participants was 1.7 and was not statistically significant; furthermore, there was only one incident case of PD exposed to rotenone, precluding a stable incidence estimate.

study_context.dose: self-reported ever-use; exposure level not quantified
study_context.route: occupational (pesticide applicators, Iowa and North Carolina)

source.quoted_span: In the AHS cohort the odds ratio for prevalent PD cases was 1.7 and not significant (Kamel et al., 2007). There was only one incident case of PD exposed to rotenone.

limitations: OR of 1.7 has wide CI and is non-significant; rotenone users too few for stable incidence estimate (n=1 incident case)
Prevalent PD case analysis may introduce survival/selection bias
Prospective design advantageous but relies on self-reported rotenone use at enrollment

objects.claim: In the Tanner et al. (2009) AHS nested case-control study (FAME), only one case and one control were exposed to rotenone, yielding an odds ratio of 0.82 (95% CI: 0.05–13.34) — a null, statistically uninformative result.

study_context.route: occupational (AHS applicators, Iowa and North Carolina)

source.quoted_span: In the Tanner et al. study there was one case and one control exposed to rotenone (OR = 0.82; 95% CI .05, 13.34) (Tanner et al., 2009).

limitations: Confidence interval spans 0.05–13.34; study is completely underpowered for rotenone-specific inference
Only 1 exposed case and 1 exposed control — single discordant pair would reverse the OR
Cannot distinguish no-effect from insufficient power to detect an effect

objects.claim: The AHS 20-year prospective cohort study (Shrestha/Sandler 2020, n=38,274 applicators + 27,836 spouses; 373 incident PD applicators) found significant incident PD associations for terbufos, trifluralin, and 2,4,5-T, but did not report rotenone as among the significant or null pesticides evaluated in the 50-pesticide panel, reflecting rotenone's very low prevalence of use in the cohort.

source.quoted_span: Ever-use of the insecticide terbufos (HR:1.31, 95%CI:1.02–1.68) and the herbicides trifluralin (HR:1.29, 95%CI: 0.99–1.70) and 2,4,5-T (HR:1.57, 95%CI:1.21–2.04) was associated with elevated PD risk.

limitations: Rotenone was not among the 50 pesticides with sufficient exposure prevalence for individual analysis, reflecting low use in this cohort
Self-reported PD diagnosis (no systematic clinical ascertainment) introduces outcome misclassification
Exposure ascertainment by self-report at enrollment may not capture post-enrollment use

objects.claim: A 2006 systematic review of epidemiological studies on pesticides and PD concluded that most case-control studies had small numbers of cases, that pesticide exposure history was collected retrospectively by questionnaire, and that information and recall bias are inherent limitations — limiting any rotenone-specific causal inference.

source.quoted_span: Pesticide exposure history was, by necessity, collected retrospectively, generally using questionnaires. Information and recall bias are inherent limitations of this type of design. The exposure assessments were also limited in their collection of information on the types of pesticides, specific chemicals, and levels of exposure experienced.

limitations: Review pre-dates the 2011 Tanner AHS nested case-control and 2020 AHS cohort study
Critique applies to case-control design broadly, not rotenone-specific studies exclusively
Authors note the association is unlikely to be explained wholly by bias, qualifying this as a limits finding

objects.claim: A New Mexico Department of Health regulatory review concluded that the epidemiological evidence for PD risk associated with specific pesticide use settings (agricultural, occupational, residential) — including botanicals such as rotenone — was 'inconclusive' and that evidence across studies was 'inconsistent and inconclusive.'

source.quoted_span: The association between PD and more specific pesticide use settings appears to be inconclusive for 1) agricultural use of pesticides, which included farming, 2) other occupational use, and 3) residential use of pesticides, which included gardening.

limitations: State-level regulatory review, not a peer-reviewed systematic review
Review covers multiple pesticides including rotenone without individual rotenone-specific analysis
Cited evidence base predates 2011 and 2020 AHS publications

objects.claim: A 2023 review in npj Parkinson's Disease noted that inconsistent findings across pesticide epidemiology studies (including for paraquat and rotenone-class agents) may be due to limited sample size, inconsistencies in exposure classification and exposure misclassification, or individual differences in genetic background.

source.quoted_span: These inconsistent findings may be due to multiple factors, for instance the limited sample size or inconsistencies in exposure classification/exposure misclassification or, perhaps most importantly, individual differences in genetic background.

limitations: Inconsistency described for pesticides broadly, not rotenone specifically
Review does not provide rotenone-specific null studies as distinct evidence
Genetic background confounding not controlled in most existing rotenone epidemiology studies

objects.claim: Epidemiological studies on rotenone and PD are hampered by the small number of individuals exposed to rotenone and other 'botanicals' compared with other pesticide classes, with early studies reporting extremely wide confidence intervals (e.g., OR=5.9, CI 0.6–56.1), making a definitive causal conclusion impossible from human data alone.

source.quoted_span: While the number of individuals exposed to rotenone and other 'botanicals' is small compared to other classes of synthetic pesticides, a recent study found an odds ratio of 5.9 (CI 0.6-56.1)7 and another found an odds ratio for rotenone of 10.9 (CI 2.5-48.0)8.

limitations: Wide CI (0.6–56.1) spans the null (OR=1.0) and is thus uninformative as to causation
Low rotenone prevalence of use in agricultural cohorts constrains all human epidemiological power
Publication bias toward positive results may further distort available OR estimates

objects.claim: A 2025 critical review in Movement Disorders noted that no meta-analyses were available for rotenone specifically, in contrast to other pesticides (paraquat, organochlorines), and that exposure misclassification in environmental epidemiology 'depends heavily on the exposure assessment methods,' limiting rotenone-specific human evidence.

pathway_edge.target_node: Parkinson's disease causal inference (meta-analytic weight of evidence)

source.quoted_span: No meta-analyses were available for other pesticides highlighted in the review, namely rotenone, glyphosate, pyrethroids, chlorpyrifos, and terbufos.

limitations: Review published 2025; may not capture all recent rotenone epidemiology data
Absence of meta-analysis reflects insufficient qualifying studies, not confirmed null effect
Covers all environmental risk factors for PD; rotenone section is not a primary focus

objects.claim: A 2025 systematic review and meta-analysis on pesticide exposure and PD highlighted that methodological heterogeneity across studies — including variations in study design, exposure evaluation methods, case definitions, and case ascertainment — may impair generalizability and weaken the level of evidence for any individual pesticide including rotenone.

pathway_edge.target_node: biased or attenuated Parkinson's disease risk estimate

source.quoted_span: The methodological heterogeneity of these studies has recently been highlighted, which may impair the generalizability of the results and weaken the level of evidence supporting the association between pesticide exposure and PD.

limitations: Preprint (medRxiv); not yet peer-reviewed
Methodological heterogeneity critique is general to pesticide epidemiology; not rotenone-specific
Meta-analysis may include predominantly positive studies due to publication bias

limitations: Study conducted in fish (rainbow trout), not mammals; human CYP450 isoform-specific metabolism not fully characterized in this source
In vitro microsomal system may not reflect in vivo metabolite ratios

source.quoted_span: Several metabolites have been identified as rotenoids (rotenolone I and II, hydroxy and dihydroxyrotenones, etc.) with a toxicity similar to rotenone. One mechanism of metabolism was demethylation.

limitations: INCHEM PIM is a secondary regulatory summary (citing Hayes 1982, Gosselin 1984); primary CYP isoform assignments not given
